name: Fermat's Little Theorem â€” Taming Big Powers
whatIsIt: FERMAT'S LITTLE THEOREM is a magic shortcut for remainders of powers. If p is a PRIME and a is not a multiple of p, then a^(pâˆ’1) â‰¡ 1 (mod p). An equivalent form: a^p â‰¡ a (mod p). So powers 'reset' to 1 every (pâˆ’1) steps, letting you find the remainder of enormous powers in seconds.
whyNeeded: It cracks 'what is the remainder of 2^100 divided by 3?' type problems instantly and is the backbone of number-theory Olympiad questions and of public-key cryptography.
realLifeUses: Finding the last digit or remainder of a huge power fast
The maths behind RSA encryption that secures the internet
Cyclic patterns that repeat every (pâˆ’1) powers

vocabulary.meaning: A number > 1 divisible only by 1 and itself; required for the theorem.
For prime p and a not divisible by p: a^(pâˆ’1) â‰¡ 1 (mod p).

story.title: The Giant Power
story.text: Vikram is stuck: 'What is 2^100 divided by 3, the remainder?' Sir smiles, 'Fermat! 3 is prime, so 2^(3âˆ’1) = 2^2 â‰¡ 1 (mod 3). And 2^100 = (2^2)^50, which is 1^50 = 1 (mod 3). Remainder 1 â€” no giant multiplication.' Vikram is amazed: 'The powers reset to 1 every 2 steps mod 3!' 'That,' says Sir, 'is Fermat's Little Theorem.'
story.extractedProblem: Using Fermat's Little Theorem, find the remainder when 2^100 is divided by 3.

visual.caption: For a prime p, raising to the (pâˆ’1) power lands you back on 1 (mod p) â€” the powers cycle and reset.

teachingGallery.title: Powers reset to 1
teachingGallery.note: a^(pâˆ’1) â‰¡ 1 (mod p) when p is prime.

standardMethod.summary: Check p is prime and a is not a multiple of p. Then a^(pâˆ’1) â‰¡ 1 (mod p). Break a big exponent into a multiple of (pâˆ’1) plus a remainder, and only the remainder part is left to compute.
steps: Confirm the modulus p is prime and a is not divisible by p.
Write the exponent as (pâˆ’1)Ã—q + r; then a^exponent â‰¡ a^r (mod p).
Compute the small leftover a^r (mod p).

workedExamples.problem: Find 2^6 mod 7 using Fermat's Little Theorem.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using the theorem when the modulus is not prime.
commonMistakes.fix: Fermat's Little Theorem needs p PRIME (and a not a multiple of p). For composite moduli use Euler's theorem instead.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing a^p â‰¡ 1 instead of a^(pâˆ’1) â‰¡ 1.
commonMistakes.fix: The exponent is p âˆ’ 1: a^(pâˆ’1) â‰¡ 1 (mod p). (Equivalently a^p â‰¡ a.)

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up how Fermat cracks 2^100 mod 3: because 3 is prime, 2^2 â‰¡ 1 (mod 3), so 2^100 = (2^2)^50 â‰¡ 1. Explain why the powers 'reset' every (pâˆ’1) steps.

revisionCard.summary: Fermat's Little Theorem: if p is PRIME and a is not a multiple of p, then a^(pâˆ’1) â‰¡ 1 (mod p) (equivalently a^p â‰¡ a). Break a big exponent into multiples of (pâˆ’1); only the leftover power remains. Needs p prime â€” else use Euler.

rememberIt.hook: Prime p: a^(pâˆ’1) â‰¡ 1 (mod p) â€” powers reset
rememberIt.unpack: Break the exponent into multiples of (pâˆ’1); only the leftover matters. Needs p prime.

studentQuestions.q: What if the modulus isn't prime?
studentQuestions.a: Then use Euler's theorem: a^Ï†(n) â‰¡ 1 (mod n) for a coprime to n, where Ï†(n) counts numbers below n coprime to it.

studentQuestions.q: How does this secure the internet?
studentQuestions.a: RSA encryption uses these modular-power resets so that messages can be scrambled and only unscrambled with a secret key.

formulas.name: Fermat's Little Theorem
formulas.formula: a^(pâˆ’1) â‰¡ 1 (mod p),  p prime, pâˆ¤a
formulas.whenToUse: Remainder of a big power with a prime modulus.
